stops.name: Colosseum
stops.shortDescription: The largest ancient amphitheater ever built, completed in 80 AD under Emperor Titus. It could hold an estimated 50,000 to 80,000 spectators for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles.

stops.name: Arch of Constantine
stops.shortDescription: A colossal triumphal arch erected in 315 AD to commemorate Constantine I's victory at the Battle of Milvian Bridge. It is notable for heavily recycling decorative materials from older imperial monuments.

stops.name: Palatine Hill
stops.shortDescription: The centermost of the Seven Hills of Rome and one of the most ancient parts of the city. According to Roman mythology, this is the very spot where the city was founded by Romulus and Remus.

stops.name: Roman Forum
stops.shortDescription: The ruined but sprawling heart of ancient Rome, filled with temples, basilicas, and public spaces. For centuries, it was the center of day-to-day life in the Roman Republic and Empire.

stops.name: Piazza del Campidoglio
stops.shortDescription: A magnificent hilltop square redesigned by Michelangelo in the 16th century. It features a striking geometric paving pattern and a replica bronze equestrian statue of Marcus Aurelius.

stops.name: Capitoline Museums
stops.shortDescription: Considered the world's oldest public museums, tracing their history back to 1471. They house incredible classical sculptures, including the famous bronze 'Capitoline Wolf' nursing Romulus and Remus.

stops.name: Trajan's Column
stops.shortDescription: A Roman triumphal column that commemorates Emperor Trajan's victory in the Dacian Wars. It is famous for its intricate, spiraling bas-relief epic narrative.

stops.name: Altare della Patria
stops.shortDescription: A massive, gleaming white marble monument built in honor of Victor Emmanuel II, the first king of a unified Italy. Locals often affectionately or jokingly call it 'The Typewriter'.

stops.name: Largo di Torre Argentina
stops.shortDescription: A sunken square containing the ruins of four Roman Republican temples. Famously, it is the exact site where Julius Caesar was assassinated in 44 BC. It now hosts a beloved cat sanctuary.

stops.name: Piazza della Minerva
stops.shortDescription: A charming piazza just behind the Pantheon. It features an adorable marble elephant statue designed by Bernini, which is carrying an ancient Egyptian obelisk on its back.

stops.name: Pantheon
stops.shortDescription: A former Roman temple, now a church, renowned for its massive unreinforced concrete dome and central oculus. It remains one of the best-preserved ancient Roman buildings in the world.

stops.name: Piazza Navona
stops.shortDescription: Built directly on the site of the ancient Stadium of Domitian, this elegant, oval-shaped piazza features magnificent fountains, including Bernini's spectacular Fountain of the Four Rivers.

stops.name: Trevi Fountain
stops.shortDescription: The largest Baroque fountain in the city and arguably the most famous fountain in the world. Tradition says tossing a coin over your left shoulder ensures your return to Rome.

stops.name: Spanish Steps
stops.shortDescription: A monumental, sweeping stairway of 135 steps connecting Piazza di Spagna to the Trinità dei Monti church. It has served as a classic meeting place and an iconic Roman landmark for centuries.
